rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Charging Order And Appointment Of Receiver
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion and Discovery Calendar for Tuesday, April 21, 2026, Line 2, PLAINTIFFS CRYSTAL LEI and BRYANT FU's Motion For Charging Order And Appointment Of Receiver. Judgment creditors Crystal Lei and Bryant Fu's motion for charging order and appointment of receiver is granted. The judgment creditors have shown good cause and statutory authority for the relief they seek by this motion. Based on the materials submitted by the moving parties, the court discredits the portions of the declaration of Demas Yan stating that Tina Yan does not have an ownership interest in the 29181 Dixon, LLC. There has been no showing why the requested charging order and appointment of receiver should not be made. The court will sign the proposed order previously submitted by the moving parties. That order provides that "A receiver shall be appointed," but requires a separate order to identify the receiver and all other material terms of the appointment. Moving parties must make an ex parte application or noticed motion for a separate order to identify the receiver and all other material terms of the appointment. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(301/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: UNIFIED ACCOUNTING & TAX, LLP, A CALIFORNIA VS. SHIFT TECHNOLOGIES, INC., A DELAWARE CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion and Discovery Calendar for Tuesday, April 21, 2026, Line 3, DEFENDANT GRETCHEN RIVERA'S DEMURRER TO AMENDED COMPLAINT. (Part 1 of 2 for purposes of entry of tentative ruling.) Defendant Gretchen Rivera's demurrer to all three causes of action alleged in the second amended complaint filed by plaintiff Unified Accounting & Tax, LLP is sustained with leave to amend as to the first and second causes of action for breach of contract and intentional interference with contractual relations and overruled as to the third cause of action for breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. Rivera has leave to file a third amended complaint no later than May 13, 2026 alleging, if she can do so in good faith, that at the time Rivera induced Picoc to work for Shift Rivera knew that Picoc had a consulting relationship with UAT as factual support for her claims for breach of contract and interference with contract. Rivera's demurrer to the first two claims does not run afoul of the prohibition in CCP 430.41 and the common law rule against demurring on a ground that could have been raised but wasn't in an earlier demurrer. While the allegations of a complaint must be liberally construed, UAT's new allegations that Rivera "intentionally remained ignorant of Picoc's ... association with UAT" (SAC pars. 41 and 55) affirmatively show, notwithstanding Rivera's alleged earlier knowledge of such an association (SAC pars. 36 and 50), that at the time of the alleged misconduct by Rivera she lacked knowledge of Picoc's [contractual] association with UAT." These new allegations, not contained in the original complaint, now permit Rivera to demur to the first two claims notwithstanding that her prior demurrer to claims with the same names had been overruled. Due to the new allegations referenced above, the first two claims do not allege sufficient ultimate facts to show either breach of the Rivera-UAT contract or knowledge by Rivera of the Picoc-UAT contract, which are essential elements of those claims. Although UAT argues that the new allegations are "complementary" and alleged in the alternative to the previous allegations, a fair reading of the SAC shows otherwise. In light of the new allegations, the previous allegations clearly refer to a time period before Rivera's alleged misconduct, not at the time of that misconduct. In all events, the new allegations of "intentional ignorance" are insufficient to support either of the first two claims. UAT's new allegations of "intentional ignorance" suffice to allege a cognizable claim of breach of the implied covenant. The key element of that claim is that the defendant's conduct prevented the plaintiff from receiving the benefits of a contract between the plaintiff and the defendant. (CACI 325.) By alleging that Rivera was intentionally ignorant of the UAT-Picoc contract yet nonetheless induced Picoc to work with Shift, UAT has sufficiently alleged this key element. Rivera's argument that UAT seeks to enforce duties outside the scope of the parties' settlement agreement, such as a duty to set up a system to check applicants' employment background, lacks merit, at least at the demurrer stage. UAT properly states a claim for relief without relying on any duties outside the scope of the settlement agreement. Rivera's alleged intentional ignorance is sufficient to constitute conduct that prevented UAT from receiving the benefits of the settlement agreement. (Tentative Ruling continues in Part 2) =(301/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: KUN WANG VS. UBER TECHNOLOGY INC., A DELAWARE CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Terminating Sanctions Against Plaintiff For Perjury Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion and Discovery Calendar for Tuesday, April 21, 2026, Line 5, 1- DEFENDANT UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC.'s Motion For Terminating Sanctions Against Plaintiff For Perjury. Defendant Uber Technologies, Inc.'s motion for terminating sanctions is denied, although lesser sanctions crafted by the court are ordered. Plaintiff Kun Wang's request for sanctions is denied. The court rejects the unsupported assertions of the plaintiff that his conduct relating to his attendance at and graduation from UC Berkeley and his grades received from San Jose City College are explainable by his alleged traumatic brain injury. Plaintiff has had sufficient opportunity to submit expert testimony supporting his assertions and his failure to do so shows that he intentionally misrepresented that he attended and graduated from UC Berkeley and received far better grades than he actually received from San Jose City College to gain an unwarranted litigation advantage. His doing so is wholly inconsistent with the truth-seeking functions of court adjudication and is strongly condemnable. But it does not, without more, warrant terminating sanctions. The court invites counsel for defendants to draft an instruction to the trier of fact regarding these matters in the context of language from CACI 107 that, based on plaintiff's acknowledged falsehoods, the trier of fact may choose not to believe some or all of plaintiff's testimony on other matters. The court also requires on a mutually convenient date no later than May 15, 2026 that plaintiff submit to an additional session of his deposition, not to exceed three hours, regarding these matters, including whether he forged or doctored any other documents and/or made any other false statements at his deposition. The costs of the additional session of plaintiff's deposition -- including the court reporter fees and time spent by defense counsel during the deposition at their normal hourly rates -- are to be borne by the plaintiff and to be paid within ten days of the additional session of the deposition. The lesser sanctions crafted by the court are tailored to remediate the discovery abuse committed by the plaintiff. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. rulings.Calendar Matter: Amended Notice Of Motion And Motion To Set Aside Order Granting Motion To Strike Pursuant To Ccp 473 And (Proposed] Order
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion and Discovery Calendar for Tuesday, April 21, 2026, Line 6, PLAINTIFFS MUSEUM PLAZA, LLC, WALID MANDO, and FORMA, LLC's Motion To Set Aside Order Granting Motion To Strike Pursuant To CCP Sec. 473. Plaintiffs' motion to set aside the February 3, 2026 order striking the complaint without leave to amend is granted. By opposing the motion on the merits, defendants waived any defects in the filing and service of the motion papers. (Carlton v. Quint (2000) 77 Cal. App. 4th 690). David Ericksen's declaration establishes plaintiffs' entitlement to the vacation of the February 3, 2026 order. The motion to strike will be heard on April 27, 2026. Plaintiffs must file their opposition papers to the motion to strike no later than April 21, 2026. Defendants may file any reply papers on their motion to strike no later than April 23, 2026. rulings.Case Title: KATHERINE TYLER VS. B&G FOODS, INC., A DELAWARE CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Defendant B&G Foods, Inc.S Further Responses To Special Interrogatories [Set One] And Request For Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion and Discovery Calendar for Tuesday, April 21, 2026, Line 8, 1- PLAINTIFF KATHERINE TYLER's Motion To Compel Defendant B&G Foods, Inc.'s Further Responses To Special Interrogatories [Set One] And Request For Monetary Sanctions. Defendant Katherine Tyler's motion to compel defendant B&G Foods, Inc. to serve further responses to her set one special interrogatories 1-5 and 21 and for monetary sanctions is granted in large part. No later than May 13, 2026 B&G shall serve verified code-compliant further responses to interrogatories 1-5 and 21 without objections and B&G and its counsel shall pay $2,080 in reasonable monetary sanctions to Tyler. If B&G believes that a protective order is required before it provides further responses, no later than May 4, 2026 B&G must submit either a stipulated proposed protective order to the court or, if the parties cannot agree on the language of a proposed protective order, apply ex parte for entry of its proposed protective order. All of B&G's objections lack merit. The six interrogatories all seek discovery relevant information and B&G has failed to substantiate any of its objections. Even if B&G cannot provide precise responses, its discovery obligations require it to provide the best information that it has available to it. Because B&G's responses lack substantial justification, Tyler is entitled to monetary sanctions. However, the amount she seeks is excessive. The court calculated the amount as reasonably incurred five hours of attorney time at $400 per hour plus $80 in claimed costs. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(301/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: KATHERINE TYLER VS. B&G FOODS, INC., A DELAWARE CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Defendant B&G Foods, Inc.S Further Responses To Requests For Production Of Documents [Set One] And Request For Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion and Discovery Calendar for Tuesday, April 21, 2026, Line 8, 2- PLAINTIFF KATHERINE TYLER's Motion To Compel Defendant B&G Foods, Inc.'s Further Responses To Requests For Production Of Documents [Set One] And Request For Monetary Sanctions. Defendant Katherine Tyler's motion to compel defendant B&G Foods, Inc. to serve further responses and produce documents responsive to her set one document requests 10, 11 and 13 and for monetary sanctions is granted in large part. No later than May 13, 2026 B&G shall serve verified code-compliant further responses to requests 10, 11 and 13 without objections other than privilege objections, produce all responsive documents other than those claimed to be privileged, and serve a privilege log as to all documents withheld on the grounds of privilege. No later than May 13, 2026 B&G and its counsel shall pay $2,080 in reasonable monetary sanctions to Tyler. If B&G believes that a protective order is required before it provides further responses or produces documents, no later than May 4, 2026 B&G must submit either a stipulated proposed protective order to the court or, if the parties cannot agree on the language of a proposed protective order, apply ex parte for entry of its proposed protective order. All of B&G's objections lack merit. The three document requests all seek discovery relevant information and B&G has failed to substantiate any of its objections. Even if B&G cannot provide precise responses, its discovery obligations require it to provide the best information that it has available to it. Because B&G's responses lack substantial justification, Tyler is entitled to monetary sanctions. However, the amount she seeks is excessive. The court calculated the amount as reasonably incurred five hours of attorney time at $400 per hour plus $80 in claimed costs. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom.
